<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I have a clear picture now. The app:
- Does NOT currently serve the frontend build from Express (only /uploads static). So we need Nginx (or add static serve in Express) for production.
- Uses MySQL, Redis, Node, React (Vite), Asterisk ARI, and optionally the asterisk-config-receiver on the Asterisk server.
- Has 26 SQL migrations to run.
- Previous spec story discussed single-server production (Asterisk + app + MySQL on one server).

For Azure Ubuntu 24.04 deployment I need to plan:
1. Azure VM / networking (NSG, public IP, domain optional)
2. Ubuntu 24.04: Node.js (LTS), MySQL 8, Redis, Nginx
3. Asterisk (if on same server) - install and configure
4. App: clone, npm install, build frontend, env config
5. Serve: Nginx reverse proxy (API + static) or Express serving dist
6. Process manager: PM2
7. SSL: Let's Encrypt (certbot)
8. Database: create DB, run schema + migrations
9. Firewall, security
10. asterisk-config-receiver: if Asterisk on same server, run on same host (port 9999)

Let me also confirm whether they want Asterisk on the same Azure VM or separate. The user said "this app" and "current full tech stack" - the stack includes Asterisk integration. I'll assume single server unless they have Asterisk elsewhere. I'll mention both options in the plan.

Creating the plan.
</details></think>

Creating a step-by-step deployment plan for Azure (Ubuntu 24.04) using the gathered tech stack and structure.


---

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="create_plan">
<details>
<summary>Tool use: **create_plan**</summary>


Parameters:

```json
{
  "plan": "# Deploy PBX Call Centre to Azure (Ubuntu 24.04)\n\n## Tech stack (from codebase)\n\n| Layer | Technology |\n|-------|------------|\n| Frontend | React 19 + Vite 7 |\n| Backend | Node.js (ESM), Express |\n| Database | MySQL 8 |\n| Session / cache | Redis (sessions + wallboard + ARI state) |\n| Telephony | Asterisk (ARI, PJSIP, queue Stasis app) |\n| Config sync | &#96;scripts/asterisk-config-receiver&#96; (Node, port 9999) |\n\nThe app does **not** serve the built frontend from Express today (only &#96;/uploads&#96;). Production will use **Nginx** to serve the Vite &#96;dist&#96; and proxy &#96;/api&#96; and &#96;/uploads&#96; to the Node server.\n\n---\n\n## Architecture (single VM)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  subgraph azure [Azure Ubuntu 24.04 VM]\n    Nginx[ Nginx 443/80 ]\n    Node[ Node app :3001 ]\n    PM2[ PM2 ]\n    MySQL[ MySQL 8 ]\n    Redis[ Redis ]\n    Asterisk[ Asterisk ARI :8088 ]\n    ConfigRx[ Config receiver :9999 ]\n    Nginx --&gt; Node\n    Node --&gt; MySQL\n    Node --&gt; Redis\n    Node --&gt; Asterisk\n    Node --&gt; ConfigRx\n  end\n  User[ User browser ] --&gt; Nginx\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n- Azure VM: Ubuntu 24.04, recommended **Standard B2s** (2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM) or larger if Asterisk + many concurrent calls.\n- A **domain name** pointed to the VM’s public IP (for HTTPS). If you only have an IP, you can still deploy with HTTP and add SSL later.\n- SSH access to the VM.\n- Code in a Git repo (GitHub/Azure DevOps/etc.) or a way to copy the project onto the VM.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 1: Azure and OS setup\n\n1. **VM and networking**\n   - Ensure the VM has a **public IP** (static recommended).\n   - In **Network security group (NSG)** allow:\n     - Inbound: 22 (SSH), 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS).\n     - If Asterisk is on this VM and you need SIP/RTP from the internet: 5060/5061 (SIP), 10000–20000 (RTP) — only open if required.\n   - (Optional) Create a **DNS A record** for your domain to the VM’s public IP.\n\n2. **Ubuntu 24.04 baseline**\n   - SSH in and update:\n     - &#96;sudo apt update &amp;&amp; sudo apt upgrade -y&#96;\n   - Set timezone: &#96;sudo timedatectl set-timezone &lt;Your/Timezone&gt;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## Phase 2: Install Node.js, MySQL, Redis, Nginx\n\n1. **Node.js 20 LTS**\n   - Use NodeSource or the official setup:\n     - &#96;curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_20.x | sudo -E bash -&#96;\n     - &#96;sudo apt install -y nodejs&#96;\n   - Verify: &#96;node -v&#96; (v20.x), &#96;npm -v&#96;.\n\n2. **MySQL 8**\n   - &#96;sudo apt install -y mysql-server&#96;\n   - &#96;sudo mysql&#96; then:\n     - &#96;ALTER USER 'root'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED WITH mysql_native_password BY 'your_strong_password';&#96;\n     - &#96;FLUSH PRIVILEGES;&#96;\n     - &#96;EXIT;&#96;\n   - Secure if desired: &#96;sudo mysql_secure_installation&#96;.\n\n3. **Redis**\n   - &#96;sudo apt install -y redis-server&#96;\n   - Enable and start: &#96;sudo systemctl enable redis-server &amp;&amp; sudo systemctl start redis-server&#96;\n   - Default: &#96;redis://127.0.0.1:6379&#96; (no auth). For production on a single VM, binding to 127.0.0.1 is fine.\n\n4. **Nginx**\n   - &#96;sudo apt install -y nginx&#96;\n   - &#96;sudo systemctl enable nginx&#96;\n\n---\n\n## Phase 3: Database and app user\n\n1. **Create database and user**\n   - &#96;mysql -u root -p&#96;\n   - Run (adjust password):\n     &#96;&#96;&#96;sql\n     CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS pbx_callcentre\n       DEFAULT CHARACTER SET utf8mb4 DEFAULT COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci;\n     CREATE USER 'pbxapp'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'strong_app_password';\n     GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON pbx_callcentre.* TO 'pbxapp'@'localhost';\n     FLUSH PRIVILEGES;\n     EXIT;\n     &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n2. **Apply schema and migrations**\n   - From project root on the server:\n     - Apply [docs/database-schema.sql](docs/database-schema.sql) (base schema).\n     - Run migrations in order from [docs/migrations/](docs/migrations/) (e.g. &#96;001_*&#96; through &#96;026_*&#96;) with &#96;mysql -u pbxapp -p pbx_callcentre &lt; docs/migrations/XXX_name.sql&#96;.\n   - Optional: use your existing seed or &#96;node server/seed.js&#96; after env is set.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 4: Deploy application code\n\n1. **Get code on the VM**\n   - Clone via Git: &#96;git clone &lt;your-repo-url&gt; /var/www/pbx-callcentre&#96; (or use &#96;scp&#96;/rsync if no repo).\n   - Use a dedicated user, e.g. &#96;pbx&#96;, and set ownership:\n     - &#96;sudo useradd -r -s /bin/bash pbx&#96; (if not exists)\n     - &#96;sudo chown -R pbx:pbx /var/www/pbx-callcentre&#96;\n\n2. **Install dependencies and build**\n   - &#96;cd /var/www/pbx-callcentre&#96;\n   - &#96;npm ci&#96; (or &#96;npm install&#96;).\n   - Build frontend with production API URL:\n     - &#96;VITE_API_URL=/ api npm run build&#96;\n     - This bakes &#96;/&#96; as the API base so the same origin is used (Nginx will serve the SPA and proxy &#96;/api&#96; to Node).\n\n3. **Environment file**\n   - Copy [.env.example](.env.example) to &#96;.env&#96; in project root and set at least:\n     - &#96;DB_HOST=localhost&#96;, &#96;DB_PORT=3306&#96;, &#96;DB_USER=pbxapp&#96;, &#96;DB_PASSWORD=...&#96;, &#96;DB_NAME=pbx_callcentre&#96;\n     - &#96;PORT=3001&#96;\n     - &#96;NODE_ENV=production&#96;\n     - &#96;CORS_ORIGIN=https://your-domain.com&#96; (or &#96;http://&lt;public-ip&gt;&#96; if no domain)\n     - &#96;SESSION_SECRET=&lt;long random string&gt;&#96;\n     - &#96;VITE_API_URL=/&#96; (only used if you rebuild; for same-origin the built app already has &#96;/&#96;)\n     - &#96;REDIS_URL=redis://127.0.0.1:6379&#96;\n     - For **Asterisk on same server**: &#96;ASTERISK_ARI_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8088&#96;, &#96;ASTERISK_APP_URL=http://127.0.0.1:3001/api/asterisk/&#96;, &#96;RECORDINGS_BASE_PATH=/var/spool/asterisk/monitor&#96;, &#96;ASTERISK_CONFIG_API_URL=http://127.0.0.1:9999&#96;\n     - For **Asterisk on another server**: set &#96;ASTERISK_ARI_URL&#96;, &#96;ASTERISK_APP_URL&#96; (URL of this app as seen from Asterisk), and &#96;ASTERISK_CONFIG_API_URL&#96; to the other server; leave &#96;RECORDINGS_BASE_PATH&#96; empty.\n   - Restrict permissions: &#96;chmod 600 .env&#96;.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 5: Run Node app with PM2\n\n1. **Install PM2**\n   - &#96;sudo npm install -g pm2&#96; (or as the &#96;pbx&#96; user with a global install in its home).\n\n2. **Start app**\n   - From &#96;/var/www/pbx-callcentre&#96;: &#96;pm2 start server/index.js --name pbx-api&#96;\n   - Or use an ecosystem file (recommended) with &#96;NODE_ENV=production&#96;, &#96;cwd&#96;, and &#96;max_memory_restart&#96;.\n\n3. **Startup on boot**\n   - &#96;pm2 startup&#96; (run the command it prints, usually with &#96;sudo&#96;)\n   - &#96;pm2 save&#96;\n\n4. **Config receiver (if Asterisk is on this VM)**\n   - &#96;cd /var/www/pbx-callcentre/scripts/asterisk-config-receiver&#96;\n   - &#96;node server.js&#96; (or run with PM2). Set &#96;PORT=9999&#96; and optional &#96;CONFIG_API_KEY&#96; via env; ensure it can write to &#96;/etc/asterisk&#96; and read Asterisk logs if used.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 6: Nginx reverse proxy and static files\n\n1. **Site config** (e.g. &#96;/etc/nginx/sites-available/pbx&#96;)\n   - Serve React build from &#96;root /var/www/pbx-callcentre/dist;&#96; with &#96;try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html;&#96; for SPA.\n   - Proxy to Node:\n     - &#96;location /api/ { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3001; proxy_http_version 1.1; proxy_set_header Host $host; proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme; }&#96;\n     - Same for &#96;location /uploads/&#96;.\n   - For WebSocket (if any): add &#96;proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; proxy_set_header Connection \"upgrade\";&#96; for the relevant path.\n\n2. **Enable and test**\n   - &#96;sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/pbx /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/&#96;\n   - Remove default site if desired: &#96;sudo rm /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default&#96;\n   - &#96;sudo nginx -t &amp;&amp; sudo systemctl reload nginx&#96;\n\n---\n\n## Phase 7: SSL with Let’s Encrypt (if you have a domain)\n\n1. **Certbot**\n   - &#96;sudo apt install -y certbot python3-certbot-nginx&#96;\n   - &#96;sudo certbot --nginx -d your-domain.com&#96;\n   - Use redirect to HTTPS when prompted.\n\n2. **Auto-renewal**\n   - &#96;sudo systemctl enable certbot.timer&#96; (usually enabled by package).\n\n---\n\n## Phase 8: Asterisk (if on the same VM)\n\n1. **Install Asterisk**\n   - Build from source or use a PPA; ensure ARI (&#96;res_stasis&#96;), PJSIP, and required modules are enabled.\n   - Configure &#96;http.conf&#96; (ARI on 8088), &#96;ari.conf&#96; (user/password), and your dialplan/Stasis app name to match &#96;ASTERISK_STASIS_APP&#96; (default &#96;queue-dashboard&#96;).\n\n2. **Config receiver**\n   - Run on 127.0.0.1:9999; ---
```


Here’s the direct answer and how to do it in Workbench.

**Short answer:**  
Run **database-schema.sql first**, then run **all migrations in order (001 → 026)**. Only running `database-schema.sql` is **not** enough.

**Why both are needed**

- **database-schema.sql** creates the database and most tables (users, tenants, sip_extensions, queues, queue_members, crm_customers, etc.) and is the main “base” schema.
- Some **tables and changes exist only in migrations**, for example:
  - **002**: `call_records` (CDR table)
  - **017**: `agent_status_log`
  - **022**: extra indexes and `tenant_id` on `queue_members`
  - **026**: username uniqueness per tenant

So if you run **only** `database-schema.sql`, you’ll be missing `call_records`, `agent_status_log`, and some indexes/columns. The app expects those.

**In MySQL Workbench**

1. **Run the base schema**
   - Open `docs/database-schema.sql`.
   - Execute the whole script (it does `CREATE DATABASE ...`, `USE pbx_callcentre`, then all `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`).

2. **Run migrations in order**
   - Open and run each file in **numerical order** (001, 002, 003, … 026), e.g.:
     - `001_agent_session_and_crm.sql`
     - `002_phase3_call_records_realtime.sql`
     - … through …
     - `026_username_unique_per_tenant.sql`
   - For each file: make sure the session is using database `pbx_callcentre` (or add `USE pbx_callcentre;` at the top if the migration doesn’t assume it). Then execute the script.

Migrations are written to be safe to run on top of the base schema (e.g. `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`, `ADD COLUMN ...` with checks). Running them in order after `database-schema.sql` is the correct and complete setup for Phase 3.

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is getting a MySQL error when running migration 014. The error is:

Error Code: 1175. You are using safe update mode and you tried to update a table without a WHERE that uses a KEY column.

This happens because the UPDATE statement uses a subquery in the JOIN (t) and MySQL's safe update mode requires that the UPDATE references a key column in the WHERE clause. The UPDATE doesn't have a WHERE that directly uses a key column - it uses ON c.id = t.id which should be a primary key, but MySQL's safe update mode can be strict about this.

There are two ways to fix this:

1. **User-side (Workbench)**: Disable safe update mode temporarily - as the error suggests, Preferences -> SQL Editor -> uncheck "Safe Updates" and reconnect. Then run the migration. Then re-enable it if desired.

2. **Code-side**: Change the migration so it works even with safe update mode. We can do this by using a different approach that satisfies safe mode - for example, using the primary key in a way MySQL accepts. One common approach is to use:
   - UPDATE with WHERE id IN (subquery) - sometimes safe mode accepts this if the subquery returns primary key values
   - Or use a temporary table
   - Or add a condition that explicitly uses the key: WHERE c.id IN (SELECT id FROM ...)

Let me look at the actual migration 014 file to see the exact statement and fix it so it works with safe updates (so the user doesn't have to change Workbench settings, and future runs are safe).
</details></think>

---


Checking the full migration 014 so we can fix it to run with MySQL safe update mode.


---

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: d:\web development\test\docs\migrations\014_fix_abandoned_call_status.sql</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The comment says "Uses primary key (id) in UPDATE so it runs in MySQL Workbench safe update mode" but MySQL's safe update mode doesn't consider the JOIN on c.id = t.id as sufficient - it wants a WHERE clause that uses a key column. The subquery in the JOIN is seen as not "key-based" enough for MySQL.

To make it work with safe update mode, we can rewrite the UPDATE to use a form MySQL accepts. Options:

1. **WHERE id IN (SELECT id ...)** - In older MySQL, you can't reference the same table in the subquery of an UPDATE. In MySQL 8 we can sometimes use a derived table. Let me check - actually in MySQL, UPDATE with JOIN on a subquery that selects from the same table can trigger 1175 because the optimizer doesn't consider the join key as "safe" when the subquery is a derived table.

2. **Multi-table UPDATE with WHERE c.id IN (...)** - If we do UPDATE call_records c SET c.status = 'abandoned' WHERE c.id IN (SELECT id FROM call_records WHERE ...), in MySQL this might still fail because the subquery references call_records and we're updating call_records. In MySQL you often get "You can't specify target table 'call_records' for update in FROM clause" unless you wrap the subquery in another select.

3. **Wrap subquery in a derived table**: UPDATE call_records c SET c.status = 'abandoned' WHERE c.id IN (SELECT id FROM (SELECT id FROM call_records WHERE answer_time IS NULL AND LOWER(TRIM(status)) IN ('completed', 'abondoned')) t); 
   This way we're using WHERE c.id IN (...) and the inner select is from a derived table, so MySQL allows it. And we're using the primary key `c.id` in the WHERE clause, which should satisfy safe update mode.

Let me propose that change. So the fix is:

```sql
USE pbx_callcentre;

UPDATE call_records
SET status = 'abandoned'
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT id FROM (
    SELECT id FROM call_records
    WHERE answer_time IS NULL
      AND LOWER(TRIM(status)) IN ('completed', 'abondoned')
  ) t
);
```

This:
- Uses WHERE id IN (...) with the primary key `id`, which satisfies safe update mode.
- Uses a derived table (SELECT id FROM (SELECT ...) t) to avoid "target table for update in FROM clause" error.

I'll make this edit to the migration file so the user can re-run it without changing Workbench settings.
</details></think>

---


Rewriting the migration so it runs with MySQL Workbench's safe update mode. Safe update requires the UPDATE to use a key column in the WHERE clause; the current JOIN form doesn't satisfy that.


---

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="edit_file_v2">

<details><summary>Tool use: **edit_file_v2** • Edit file: d:\web development\test\docs\migrations\014_fix_abandoned_call_status.sql</summary>

</details>

</tool-use>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The fix is in. The rewritten form:
1. Uses `WHERE id IN (...)` so the UPDATE is clearly key-based (primary key `id`) — satisfies safe update mode.
2. Wraps the inner `SELECT id FROM call_records ...` in `SELECT id FROM (...) t` so MySQL doesn’t complain about updating the same table that appears in the subquery.

The user can re-run the migration file in Workbench now.
</details></think>

---


Summary of what was wrong and what was changed:

**Cause:** With “Safe Updates” on, MySQL only allows `UPDATE`/`DELETE` when the row set is identified using a key (e.g. `WHERE id = ...` or `WHERE id IN (...)`). Your original update used `INNER JOIN (SELECT id FROM call_records ...) t ON c.id = t.id`, which the server doesn’t treat as key-based, so it raised error 1175.

**Change:** The migration was rewritten to use a key-based `WHERE` and an extra subquery so the same table isn’t updated from a direct subquery:

- `UPDATE call_records SET status = 'abandoned' WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM (SELECT id FROM call_records WHERE ...) t)`

So:

1. The update is clearly key-based (`WHERE id IN (...)`), so it’s allowed with safe update mode.
2. The inner `SELECT id FROM call_records ...` is wrapped in `SELECT id FROM (...) t` so MySQL doesn’t complain about updating `call_records` while selecting from it in the same statement.

**What you should do:** Run the updated `014_fix_abandoned_call_status.sql` again in Workbench (with safe update mode on). It should run without error 1175. No need to change Preferences.

1. **Values it expects**: `'abandoned'`, `'abondoned'` (typo), `'completed'`, `'answered'`, `'failed'`, etc. The app already handles both `'abandoned'` and `'abondoned'` in many places (e.g. `LOWER(TRIM(cr.status)) IN ('abandoned','abondoned')`).

2. **What the migration does**: It only *changes* rows that currently have `status IN ('completed', 'abondoned')` and `answer_time IS NULL` to `status = 'abandoned'`. So we're correcting bad data (wrong status for unanswered calls) to the canonical value `'abandoned'`.

3. **Effect of the rewrite**: The new SQL does **exactly the same update**: same rows, same new value. So after the migration runs (either the old or the new version), the database ends up in the same state. The app doesn't care how we wrote the UPDATE—it only cares about the final data. And the app already treats both 'abandoned' and 'abondoned' in queries, and writes 'abandoned' in call-handler.js. So:
   - No schema change (same table, same column).
   - Same set of rows updated.
   - Same value written ('abandoned').

---


Yes. The change does **not** affect any current functions or features of the app.

**1. What the migration does (unchanged)**  
It’s a one-time data fix: it finds rows in `call_records` where the call was never answered (`answer_time IS NULL`) but `status` is `'completed'` or the typo `'abondoned'`, and sets their `status` to `'abandoned'`. Only the way the UPDATE is written was changed, not what it updates or what value it sets.

**2. Same rows, same result**  
- **Before (JOIN form):** update rows where `id` is in the set of ids from the subquery.  
- **After (WHERE id IN form):** same set of ids, same column `status`, same value `'abandoned'`.  

So the final data after running the migration is the same in both cases.

**3. How the app uses `call_records.status`**  
The app only cares about the **data** in `call_records`, not how the migration is written. It already:

- Treats `'abandoned'` as the correct status for unanswered/missed calls (reports, wallboard, CDR, health).
- Handles the old typo `'abondoned'` in many queries (e.g. `LOWER(TRIM(status)) IN ('abandoned','abondoned')`).
- Writes `'abandoned'` for new abandoned calls in `call-handler.js`.

So after this migration runs (with the new syntax), the app continues to behave the same: same reports, same CDR filters, same wallboard counts, same health metrics.

**4. No schema or runtime dependency**  
- No table or column structure was changed.  
- The app does not execute this migration; it’s run once (e.g. in Workbench).  
- No application code depends on the migration file’s text, only on the resulting `call_records` rows.

**Summary:** The edit only makes the UPDATE acceptable under MySQL’s safe update mode. The set of updated rows and the value written are identical, so current app behavior is unchanged.

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is still getting error 1175 even with the rewritten query. MySQL's safe update mode is strict: it may not consider `WHERE id IN (subquery)` as "using a key column" when the subquery references the same table being updated. Some versions of MySQL/Workbench are very strict about this.

Options to fix:

1. **Use a session variable to disable safe updates only for this statement** – Run `SET SESSION SQL_SAFE_UPDATES = 0;` before the UPDATE, then the UPDATE, then `SET SESSION SQL_SAFE_UPDATES = 1;` after. This keeps the migration self-contained and doesn't require the user to change Workbench preferences. The migration file would include these SET commands.

2. **Use a temporary table** – Write the IDs to a temp table, then UPDATE ... WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM temp_table). The UPDATE then uses WHERE id IN (select from a different table), which might satisfy safe mode in some versions. Actually, I'm not sure if that always works.

3. **Document that the user must disable safe updates for this one script** – Not ideal.

The most reliable approach that keeps the migration runnable in Workbench without changing global preferences is to add the session commands to the migration file itself:

```sql
USE pbx_callcentre;

SET SESSION SQL_SAFE_UPDATES = 0;

UPDATE call_records
SET status = 'abandoned'
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT id FROM (
    SELECT id FROM call_records
    WHERE answer_time IS NULL
      AND LOWER(TRIM(status)) IN ('completed', 'abondoned')
  ) t
);

SET SESSION SQL_SAFE_UPDATES = 1;
```

This way:
- The user just runs the script as-is.
- Safe updates are turned off only for the current session and only for the duration of this script.
- After the script, safe updates is turned back on (so the next query in the same session is still protected, and other sessions are unaffected).

I'll update the migration file with this approach.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The warning "1305 PROCEDURE pbx_callcentre._add_col_if_missing does not exist" happens on the first `DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS _add_col_if_missing` because on first run the procedure doesn't exist. In MySQL, `DROP ... IF EXISTS` for routines (procedures/functions) can still generate a warning in some versions when the object doesn't exist.

To avoid the warning we have two options:

1. **Check if the procedure exists before dropping** – use a conditional drop via dynamic SQL. In MySQL we can do something like:
   - Get count of procedures named _add_col_if_missing in information_schema.routines, and only DROP if count > 0. This requires a stored procedure or multiple statements. Actually we could do:
   ```sql
   SET @cnt = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM information_schema.ROUTINES WHERE ROUTINE_SCHEMA = DATABASE() AND ROUTINE_NAME = '_add_col_if_missing');
   -- Then we need to DROP only if @cnt > 0. But we can't do conditional DROP in plain SQL without a procedure. So we'd need a small anonymous block or we use a different approach.
   ```

2. **Use a single CREATE that replaces** – MySQL doesn't have CREATE OR REPLACE for procedures. So we need DROP then CREATE.

3. **Suppress the warning** – In MySQL we can use a trick: run the DROP in a way that doesn't report the warning. One option is to wrap in a stored procedure that does the drop and ignores the warning... that's circular.

4. **Don't DROP at the start; only DROP at the end** – On first run there's no procedure, so we only need to CREATE. On re-run we need to DROP first so CREATE succeeds. So the warning only happens on first run when we DROP and it doesn't exist. The fix: use a conditional creation. We could:
   - Check if procedure exists; if not, create it. If exists, drop and create. That requires a procedure to do the check... 
   - Simpler: just remove the first DROP. Then on first run we CREATE (succeeds). On second run CREATE would fail with "procedure already exists". So we do need the first DROP for re-runnability. The issue is just the warning.

5. **MySQL documentation**: In MySQL 5.7+, DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS is supposed to not error. But some clients (like Workbench) still show a "warning" (1 warning). So the migration actually succeeds; it's just a cosmetic warning.

The cleanest fix that works in all cases: use dynamic SQL to only DROP when the procedure exists. We can do that with a single block:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) INTO @_cnt FROM information_schema.ROUTINES 
WHERE ROUTINE_SCHEMA = DATABASE() AND ROUTINE_NAME = '_add_col_if_missing';
-- But we can't run DROP conditionally in one script without a procedure.

-- Alternative: use a stored block (anonymous block). MySQL 8 has anonymous blocks with BEGIN...END in a DO or in a compound statement.
```

In MySQL 8 we can use a stored program to conditionally drop:

```sql
DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS _add_col_if_missing;
```
Actually the simplest fix is to add a line that suppresses the warning for that statement. In MySQL there isn't a direct "suppress warning" but we could:
- Use a different approach: create a temporary procedure that does nothing, so that when we DROP, we're dropping something? No, that doesn't help the first run.

Actually, the best approach is to use conditional logic in MySQL 8. We can do:

```sql
-- Only drop if it exists to avoid 1305 warning in Workbench
SET @drop_proc = (SELECT IF(
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM information_schema.ROUTINES WHERE ROUTINE_SCHEMA = DATABASE() AND ROUTINE_NAME = '_add_col_if_missing') > 0,
  'DROP PROCEDURE _add_col_if_missing',
  'SELECT 1'
));
PREPARE stmt FROM @drop_proc;
EXECUTE stmt;
DEALLOCATE PREPARE stmt;
```

So we build a string that is either 'DROP PROCEDURE _add_col_if_missing' or 'SELECT 1', then execute it. When the procedure doesn't exist we execute SELECT 1 (no-op). When it exists we execute DROP. No warning. Let me add that to the migration file and also keep the final DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS (or we could use the same conditional for the final drop - but the final drop is after we've created and used the procedure, so it will always exist at that point, so DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS at the end shouldn't warn). Actually the end DROP will find the procedure (we just created it), so no warning there. So only the first DROP causes the warning. I'll replace the first DROP with the conditional drop.
</details></think>

Wait - the migration file uses DELIMITER $$ which is a client directive, not sent to the server. When you run the script, the PREPARE/EXECUTE/DEALLOCATE are run as separate statements. But then the next line is "DELIMITER $$" - in MySQL Workbench, the DELIMITER command changes how the client splits statements. So the flow is:

1. Run SET @_drop = ...
2. Run PREPARE stmt FROM @_drop; EXECUTE stmt; DEALLOCATE PREPARE stmt;
3. DELIMITER $$  (client only)
4. CREATE PROCEDURE ... END$$  (one block)
5. DELIMITER ;
6. CALL _add_col_if_missing(...)
7. DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS _add_col_if_missing;

One potential issue: in some MySQL versions or strict mode, PREPARE stmt FROM @_drop might have issues if the variable has a multi-statement or something. Here @_drop is either 'DROP PROCEDURE _add_col_if_missing' or 'SELECT 1', both single statements. So we're good.

I'll leave the file as is. The user can re-run the migration. If they already ran it and the procedure was created, the conditional will find it and drop it, then CREATE will run again. If they're running fresh, the conditional will do SELECT 1, then CREATE will run. No warning.

Summary of what was changed:

**What was going on**  
`DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS _add_col_if_missing` is valid, but on the **first run** the procedure doesn’t exist. In that case MySQL still raises warning **1305** (“procedure does not exist”) even with `IF EXISTS`. The migration still runs; the issue is only the warning.

**What was done**  
The initial unconditional `DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS` was replaced with a **conditional drop** that only runs when the procedure exists:

1. A query checks `information_schema.ROUTINES` for a routine named `_add_col_if_missing` in the current database.
2. If it exists, the code executes `DROP PROCEDURE _add_col_if_missing`.
3. If it doesn’t exist, the code executes `SELECT 1` (no-op).

So:

- **First run:** procedure doesn’t exist → no drop, no 1305 warning → `CREATE PROCEDURE` runs.
- **Re-run:** procedure exists → it is dropped → `CREATE PROCEDURE` runs again.

The rest of the migration (creating the procedure, calling it to add columns, final `DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS`) is unchanged. You can re-run the whole migration 020 script; the 1305 warning should no longer appear.
